title: Vulnerability Assessment Methodology
description: Systematically find, test, and document security vulnerabilities in any web application using a repeatable step-by-step process
learning_outcomes: Map a web application's complete attack surface systematically
Test every input field for injection vulnerabilities (XSS, SQLi)
Inspect cookies, headers, and session management for flaws
Check access controls by testing URLs across different user roles
Document findings with CWE references and CIA impact analysis
Write professional vulnerability reports with evidence

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Vulnerability hunting is like being a detective. Want me to give you the first clue to look for?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? Let me simplify: start with the login form. Type a single quote. What happens? That one test can reveal SQL injection.
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush. Let me walk you through a complete example — testing one form field from start to finish.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Good attempt! Remember: we test ONE thing at a time. Check if the input even reaches the server first.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: Let me show you the checklist approach. We go through each test mechanically — no guessing needed.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Excellent find! You just discovered a vulnerability that could affect real users. Now document it with the CWE number.

encouragement_phrases: You're thinking like a security auditor now!
That's exactly how professional penetration testers work.
Every form field you test is a potential discovery.
Bug bounty hunters earn thousands for finds like this.
Systematic testing beats random guessing every time.
You just found something most developers miss!
Documentation is what separates amateurs from professionals.
Real security audits follow exactly this methodology.

explanation_styles.analogy: Testing a web app is like checking every door and window of a building. You walk the perimeter, try each handle, and note which ones are unlocked. Systematic, thorough, no shortcuts.
explanation_styles.visual: Attack Surface Map: [Login Form] → test SQLi, XSS | [Profile Form] → test XSS, CSRF | [Transfer Form] → test CSRF | [Cookies] → check flags | [URLs] → check access control
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: List all pages. Step 2: List all forms. Step 3: List all input fields. Step 4: Test each field. Step 5: Check cookies. Step 6: Check access controls. Step 7: Document everything.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Input: ' OR '1'='1 → Goes into SQL query → WHERE password='' OR '1'='1' → Always TRUE → Authentication bypassed
explanation_styles.comparison: Random testing: try stuff, hope for the best, miss most bugs. Systematic testing: follow a checklist, test everything, find ALL the bugs. Same time, 10x more results.

lessons.title: Vulnerability Assessment — Systematic Security Testing from Zero to Report

theory.introduction:
Imagine you're a home inspector. You don't just walk in, glance around, and say 'looks fine.' You have a checklist: check the foundation, the roof, the plumbing, the electrical, the insulation — every system, methodically.

Vulnerability assessment works the same way. You don't randomly poke at a web application hoping something breaks. You follow a systematic process: map the application, identify every input point, test each one against known vulnerability types, and document what you find.

This module gives you that process. By the end, you'll have a repeatable methodology you can apply to ANY web application — not just the one you're testing today. Whether you're doing a university assessment, a bug bounty, or a professional penetration test, the steps are the same.

The difference between a beginner and a professional isn't talent — it's methodology. Professionals find more bugs because they test more thoroughly, not because they're smarter.
theory.why_important:
Random testing misses vulnerabilities. A systematic approach ensures you check EVERYTHING:

Without methodology: 'I tried SQL injection on the login and it didn't work, so the app is secure.' (Wrong — you only tested ONE field on ONE page.)

With methodology: 'I tested all 12 input fields across 5 pages for SQLi, XSS, and command injection. I checked all cookies for security flags. I tested all URLs for access control. Here are the 5 vulnerabilities I found.' (Professional.)

The methodology also ensures your documentation is complete — which is critical for assessment, bug bounty reports, and professional security assessments.

key_concepts.explanation:
Before testing anything, you need to know WHAT to test. Reconnaissance means exploring every page, every form, every link in the application.

Create an Attack Surface Map:
1. Visit every page as each user role (admin, normal user, not logged in)
2. Write down every URL
3. List every form and its input fields
4. Note every link in the navigation
5. Check the HTML source for hidden fields, comments, and JavaScript files
6. Open Developer Tools (F12) and check Network tab while browsing

This map becomes your testing checklist. Every item on it gets tested.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The attack surface map is your treasure map. Every X marks a potential vulnerability. The more thorough your map, the more treasures you'll find.

key_concepts.explanation: Every input field is a potential injection point. Test each one systematically: SQL Injection test: 1. Enter a single quote: ' 2. Enter: ' OR '1'='1' -- 3. Enter: ' OR 'x'='x 4. Watch for: error messages, unexpected access, data leakage XSS test: 1. Enter: 2. Enter: 3. Enter: "> 4. Check: does the input appear in the page? Is it encoded? Command Injection test: 1. Enter: ; ls 2. Enter: | whoami 3. Enter: && cat /etc/passwd 4. Watch for: system output in responses Test EVERY field, not just the obvious ones.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Test like a toddler exploring a room — touch everything, push every button, open every drawer. But write down what happens each time.

key_concepts.explanation:
Session management is one of the most common vulnerability areas. Check:

1. Cookie flags (F12 → Storage/Application → Cookies):
   - HttpOnly: Can JavaScript read the cookie? (should be true)
   - Secure: Is cookie sent over HTTP? (should be true)
   - SameSite: Is cookie sent cross-site? (should be Strict or Lax)

2. Session behaviour:
   - Does the session ID change after login? (should change)
   - Does logout actually destroy the session? (should invalidate)
   - How long until the session expires? (should be reasonable)

3. Cookie content:
   - Is the session ID random and long? (should be unpredictable)
   - Does the cookie contain sensitive data? (should NOT)
key_concepts.ai_hint: Checking cookie flags takes 30 seconds but can reveal critical vulnerabilities. It's the highest-value, lowest-effort test in your toolkit.

key_concepts.explanation:
Access control bugs are among the most common and most impactful vulnerabilities. Test:

1. Horizontal access control (same role, different users):
   - Log in as user1, note the URL: /account?username=user1
   - Change to: /account?username=user2
   - Can you see user2's data? → IDOR (CWE-639)

2. Vertical access control (different roles):
   - Log in as normal user
   - Try accessing admin URLs: /balance, /admin, /users
   - Can you access admin pages? → Missing authorisation (CWE-862)

3. Function-level access:
   - Can a normal user perform admin actions via direct POST?
   - Remove authentication cookies — can you still access pages?
key_concepts.ai_hint: Access control testing is like checking if a hotel key card opens rooms it shouldn't. Log in as a regular guest, then try opening the manager's office door.

key_concepts.explanation:
For every form that performs an action (transfer, update, delete), check:

1. Does the form have a hidden CSRF token field?
   - Right-click → Inspect → look for <input type='hidden' name='csrf_token'>
   - If NO token → vulnerable to CSRF (CWE-352)

2. Can you replay the form from an external page?
   - Create an HTML file with a hidden form pointing to the target
   - Add auto-submit JavaScript
   - Open it while logged into the application
   - If the action succeeds → CSRF confirmed

3. Check the Network tab (F12):
   - Submit the form normally
   - Look at the POST request parameters
   - Is there any token or nonce? Or just the data fields?
key_concepts.ai_hint: No CSRF token = anyone can submit that form on behalf of any logged-in user. Check EVERY form that does something important.

key_concepts.concept: Phase 6: Error Handling and Information Disclosure
key_concepts.explanation:
Applications often leak information through error messages. Test:

1. Trigger errors with invalid input:
   - Enter a quote (') in form fields → SQL error?
   - Request non-existent pages → stack trace?
   - Submit empty required fields → exception details?
   - Upload wrong file types → internal error messages?

2. Check what the error reveals:
   - Database type (MySQL, SQLite, Oracle)
   - Server technology (Jetty, Tomcat, Apache)
   - File paths (/opt/app/src/Servlet.java)
   - Class names (AccountPageServlet, Database.java)
   - Query structure (SELECT * FROM users WHERE...)

3. Check HTML source:
   - HTML comments with TODOs or debug info
   - JavaScript files with API endpoints
   - Hidden form fields with internal data
key_concepts.ai_hint: Error messages are like a chatty receptionist who tells you the building's alarm code when you ask for directions. The information seems harmless but helps an attacker plan their real attack.

key_concepts.explanation:
Every vulnerability needs proper documentation:

1. Name and CWE number:
   - SQL Injection → CWE-89
   - Stored XSS → CWE-79
   - CSRF → CWE-352
   - Missing authorisation → CWE-862
   - Info disclosure → CWE-209
   - Insecure cookies → CWE-614, CWE-1004

2. OWASP Top 10 category:
   - A01: Broken Access Control
   - A02: Cryptographic Failures
   - A03: Injection (SQLi, XSS, Command Injection)
   - A05: Security Misconfiguration
   - A07: Authentication Failures

3. CIA Impact:
   - Confidentiality: Can attacker see private data?
   - Integrity: Can attacker modify data?
   - Availability: Can attacker disrupt the service?
key_concepts.ai_hint: Good documentation is what gets you marks in assessments and gets you paid in bug bounties. Same finding, 10x more value with proper write-up.

key_concepts.explanation:
Use this checklist for every web application you test:

RECONNAISSANCE:
[ ] List all pages and URLs
[ ] List all forms and input fields
[ ] Check HTML source for comments and hidden fields
[ ] Identify JavaScript files and API endpoints
[ ] Map user roles and permissions

INJECTION TESTING:
[ ] Test every input field with: ' (single quote)
[ ] Test every input field with: <script>alert(1)</script>
[ ] Test every input field with: ; ls or | whoami
[ ] Check if inputs are reflected in responses (reflected XSS)
[ ] Check if inputs are stored and displayed later (stored XSS)

SESSION TESTING:
[ ] Check HttpOnly flag on session cookie
[ ] Check Secure flag
[ ] Check SameSite attribute
[ ] Test session regeneration on login
[ ] Test session destruction on logout

ACCESS CONTROL:
[ ] Test horizontal access (user1 → user2's data)
[ ] Test vertical access (user → admin pages)
[ ] Test without authentication (direct URL access)

CSRF:
[ ] Check every action form for CSRF tokens
[ ] Attempt auto-submit from external page

ERROR HANDLING:
[ ] Trigger errors with invalid input
[ ] Check for stack traces and version info
[ ] Check response headers for server details
key_concepts.ai_hint: Print this checklist and keep it next to your keyboard. Professional pen testers have checklists too — it's not cheating, it's being thorough.

key_concepts.explanation:
The most frequently encountered CWEs in web application testing:

Injection:
• CWE-79: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
• CWE-89: SQL Injection
• CWE-78: OS Command Injection
• CWE-22: Path Traversal

Authentication & Sessions:
• CWE-287: Improper Authentication
• CWE-384: Session Fixation
• CWE-614: Cookie without Secure flag
• CWE-1004: Cookie without HttpOnly flag

Access Control:
• CWE-862: Missing Authorisation
• CWE-639: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)
• CWE-269: Improper Privilege Management

Other:
• CWE-352: Cross-Site Request Forgery
• CWE-209: Error Message Information Disclosure
• CWE-502: Insecure Deserialization
• CWE-434: Unrestricted File Upload
key_concepts.ai_hint: You don't need to memorise all CWE numbers — just the top 10. They cover 90% of what you'll find in web applications.

key_concepts.explanation:
For every vulnerability, assess its impact on the CIA triad:

Confidentiality — Can the attacker SEE private data?
• HIGH: Full database access, all user records exposed
• MEDIUM: Some user data visible (names, emails)
• LOW: Only technical info leaked (server version)

Integrity — Can the attacker CHANGE data?
• HIGH: Modify accounts, transfer funds, alter records
• MEDIUM: Change non-critical data (profile descriptions)
• LOW: No direct modification possible

Availability — Can the attacker BREAK the service?
• HIGH: Database destruction, server crash
• MEDIUM: Some features disrupted
• LOW: Minor inconvenience, service continues

The combination determines the overall severity. HIGH/HIGH/LOW is Critical. LOW/LOW/LOW is Informational.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of CIA like this: Confidentiality = can they READ your diary? Integrity = can they WRITE in your diary? Availability = can they BURN your diary?

key_concepts.explanation:
A professional vulnerability report has this structure for each finding:

1. Title: [CWE number] — [Vulnerability name]
2. Severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
3. Location: Which page, form, or feature
4. Description: 2-3 sentences explaining the vulnerability
5. Steps to reproduce: Numbered list, specific and repeatable
6. Evidence: Screenshots showing each step and the result
7. Impact: CIA analysis
8. References: CWE URL, OWASP category

Tips for good reports:
• Be specific — 'the profile description field' not 'an input field'
• Include the EXACT input that triggers the vulnerability
• Screenshots should show BEFORE and AFTER
• Always include the CWE number
• Keep descriptions short — evidence speaks louder than words
key_concepts.ai_hint: A vulnerability without a good report is like finding gold but not marking the location on the map. Document everything — your future self will thank you.

key_concepts.explanation:
Essential tools for vulnerability assessment:

Built into every browser (F12):
• Elements tab: View and edit HTML source
• Console tab: Run JavaScript, see errors
• Network tab: See all HTTP requests/responses
• Storage tab: View and edit cookies

Command line:
• curl: Make HTTP requests with custom headers/cookies
• python3 -m http.server: Capture exfiltrated data
• grep: Search through responses for patterns
• nano/vim: Create attack files (csrf_attack.html)

You do NOT need expensive tools. Everything in this course can be done with a browser and a terminal. Professional tools (Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP) are helpful but not required.
key_concepts.ai_hint: F12 is your X-ray vision. It lets you see what the page is REALLY doing behind the pretty interface. Use it on every page you test.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Map a Login Page
complete_examples.description: Examine a login page and list all testable elements.
complete_examples.output: Attack surface mapped: 2 input fields, POST method, no CSRF token, no hidden fields
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Always start here. The login page reveals the authentication method, input handling, and presence/absence of CSRF protection.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Test for SQL Injection
complete_examples.description: Use a single quote to test the login form.
complete_examples.output: Login succeeds with ' OR 'x'='x → SQL Injection confirmed → CWE-89
complete_examples.ai_explanation: A single quote is the universal SQL injection test. If it causes an error or unusual behaviour, dig deeper.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Test for Stored XSS
complete_examples.description: Inject a script into a profile field and check if it executes.
complete_examples.output: Alert popup appears on page refresh → Stored XSS confirmed → CWE-79
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Stored XSS is the most dangerous because it affects EVERY visitor. Always test text fields that save to database.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Inspect Cookie Security
complete_examples.description: Check session cookie flags using Developer Tools.
complete_examples.output: Cookie flags inspected — HttpOnly=false, Secure=false, SameSite=empty → three vulnerabilities found in 30 seconds
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This 30-second test reveals three potential vulnerabilities. It's the highest-ROI test in your methodology.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Test Access Controls
complete_examples.description: Check if a normal user can access admin pages.
complete_examples.output: Normal user accesses admin page → CWE-862. User1 sees user2 data → CWE-639.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Access control bugs are in OWASP #1 for a reason — they're the most common and most impactful vulnerability category.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Test for CSRF
complete_examples.description: Check if a transfer form has CSRF protection.
complete_examples.output: Form has no CSRF token + auto-submit works → CWE-352 confirmed
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Every form that performs an action (transfer, delete, update) needs a CSRF token. If it's missing, that's a vulnerability.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Trigger Error Messages
complete_examples.description: Force the application to reveal internal information.
complete_examples.output: Error messages reveal SQL structure, server version, class names → CWE-209
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Developers often forget to configure custom error pages. Default error pages leak everything an attacker needs to know.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Document a Finding
complete_examples.description: Write a complete vulnerability report entry.
complete_examples.output: Complete vulnerability report with CWE, steps, evidence placeholders, and CIA analysis
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This template works for any vulnerability. Fill it in during testing so you don't forget details.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Full Assessment: Login Page
complete_examples.description: Complete a full assessment of just the login page.
complete_examples.output: Complete login page assessment with all tests documented and findings noted
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is how you approach every page. Systematic, thorough, documented.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Full Assessment: Transfer Page
complete_examples.description: Complete assessment of the fund transfer feature.
complete_examples.output: Complete transfer page assessment — CSRF, input validation, access control all tested
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Financial features deserve the most thorough testing. Every vulnerability here has direct monetary impact.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Compare Your Findings
complete_examples.description: Cross-reference findings to identify attack chains.
complete_examples.output: Chains identified: XSS+Cookie+Session, XSS+CSRF, SQLi+Access Control. Output encoding breaks the most chains.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Individual vulnerabilities are concerning. Chains are devastating. Always look for connections between findings.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Write the Executive Summary
complete_examples.description: Summarise all findings for a non-technical audience.
complete_examples.output: Executive summary: 5 vulns found, SQLi most critical, worm risk from chaining, output encoding is priority fix
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The executive summary is what managers and decision-makers read. Keep it short, clear, and focused on business impact.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you adapt this technique to test a different type of web application?

visual_diagram.title: Vulnerability Assessment — 7-Phase Methodology
visual_diagram.content:
VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
PHASE 1 RECONNAISSANCE
▶ List ALL pages and URLs ▶ List ALL forms and input fields ▶ Check HTML source (Ctrl+U) for comments ▶ Map user roles (admin vs normal vs anonymous) ▶ Output: Attack Surface Map
↓
PHASE 2 INJECTION TESTING
▶ Every field: type ' (single quote) → SQL error? → CWE-89 ▶ Every field: type <script>alert(1)</script> → popup? → CWE-79 ▶ Every field: type ; ls → file listing? → CWE-78
↓
PHASE 3 SESSION ANALYSIS
▶ F12 → Storage → Cookies → check flags ▶ HttpOnly = false? → CWE-1004 ▶ Secure = false? → CWE-614 ▶ Console: document.cookie → shows JSESSIONID? → stealable!
↓
PHASE 4 ACCESS CONTROL
▶ As user1: try /account?username=user2 → see their data? → CWE-639 ▶ As normal user: try admin URLs → access granted? → CWE-862
↓
PHASE 5 CSRF TESTING
▶ Inspect every action form → hidden csrf_token field? ▶ No token? → create auto-submit HTML → test → CWE-352
↓
PHASE 6 ERROR HANDLING
▶ Trigger errors with invalid input → stack trace? → CWE-209 ▶ Check source code → Java comments visible? → CWE-615 ▶ Check headers → server version exposed? → info disclosure
↓
PHASE 7 DOCUMENTATION
▶ For each finding: Name + CWE + Steps + Screenshots + CIA impact ▶ OWASP Top 10 category ▶ Reference links to cwe.mitre.org
Remember: Systematic beats random. Follow all 7 phases for EVERY application. The checklist finds bugs. Skipping steps misses bugs.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: List All Pages
practice_exercises.description: Map every URL in the application.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Welcome to Reconnaissance! Follow the methodology step by step.
practice_exercises.expected_output: Login, profile, transfer, balance (admin), logout — list every page you can access

hints.message: Start by reading the TODO markers carefully. In Reconnaissance, the key is to be systematic — don't skip any step. Think about what information you're trying to find.
Compare what you see with what you expected. If the application behaves differently from what a secure app should do, you've found something. Document it immediately with the exact input you used.
Expected: Login, profile, transfer, balance (admin), logout — list every page you can access

practice_exercises.description: Find every form and its input fields.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Welcome to Reconnaissance! Follow the methodology step by step.
